coredata.dc:title: Engineering carbon dots with a 18-crown-6 for the fluorescence detection of cadmium ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Saberi, Zeinab
Rezaei, Behzad
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with their bright photoluminescence represent a platform for the development of analytical sensors. In the context of sensing applications, there has been great interest in developing CD-based sensing platform for the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous samples. Compared to other metals, the sensing of cadmium (II) ions using carbon dots-based system has been poorly investigated. This work details the use of CDs as fluorescent probes for the sensitive detection of cadmium (II) ions. We demonstrate that the rational engineering of the surface of the CDs though the post-synthetic modification with a crown ether increases the selectivity toward the analyte via the formation of a metal-crown complex. Using this sensor, Cd2+ ions could be determined in the range from 1.0 to120 µM with 0.05 µM as a detection limit. The hybrid material shows high selectivity for cadmium ions over other ions and was validated by determination of Cd+2 in (spiked) water samples to underpin its practicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cadmium (II) ions
Crown
A fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the hybrid material
Performance of the hybrid material for the detection of cadmium (II) ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, Research and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science Research and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The dots and materials were produced at the University of Trieste in the group of Prof. Maurizio Prato. The authors would like also to thank him and his group especially Francesca Arcudi and Michele Cacioppo. The IUT group greatly acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology of I.R. Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme for the synthesis of CDs-Crown by covalent coupling of CDs-COOH and Crown-NH2.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-Crown in water at different excitation wavelengths.
Tapping mode AFM images and high profiles of (a) CDs and (b) CDs-Crown; Size histograms of (c) CDs and (d) CDs-Crown.

Optimization of the sensitivity of the fluorescence detection of CDs-Crown (1.0 mg mL−1) in presence of Cd2+ ions in water (a) through variation of pH (4–10, 100 μM Cd2+ ions) and (b) through variation of reaction time (0–14 min, 80 μM Cd2+ ions). Fluorescence intensity values were recorded from the emission spectra at 360 nm (excitation wavelength = 300 nm).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Crown (1.0 mg mL−1) and CDs-COOH (1.0 mg mL−1) in pH 7 and in presence of 100 μM Cd2+ ions at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Crown in water (pH 7) with increasing concentrations of Cd2+ ions (1.0–120 μM) (excitation wavelength = 300 nm) (b). The linear fitting curve of the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I versus the concentration of Cd2+ ions (c).

Selectivity of the hybrid material CDs-Crown in presence of 100 μM of Cd2+ ions or other metal ions (K+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+). Error bars indicate standard error of the mean, calculated from three independent experiments.
Sensing parameters of Cd2+ ions detection in real water samples. R = recovery percentage of Cd2+ ions detected related to the added amount – RSD = relative standard deviation.
Sensing parameters of this work compared to other representative Cd2+ sensors.

head.ce:title: Engineering carbon dots with a 18-crown-6 for the fluorescence detection of cadmium ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111 I.R., Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111 I.R., Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, INSTM UdR Trieste, University of Trieste, Via Licio Giorgieri 1, 34127 Trieste, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, INSTM UdR Trieste, University of Trieste, Via Licio Giorgieri 1, 34127 Trieste, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with their bright photoluminescence represent a platform for the development of analytical sensors. In the context of sensing applications, there has been great interest in developing CD-based sensing platform for the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous samples. Compared to other metals, the sensing of cadmium (II) ions using carbon dots-based system has been poorly investigated. This work details the use of CDs as fluorescent probes for the sensitive detection of cadmium (II) ions. We demonstrate that the rational engineering of the surface of the CDs though the post-synthetic modification with a crown ether increases the selectivity toward the analyte via the formation of a metal-crown complex. Using this sensor, Cd2+ ions could be determined in the range from 1.0 to120 µM with 0.05 µM as a detection limit. The hybrid material shows high selectivity for cadmium ions over other ions and was validated by determination of Cd+2 in (spiked) water samples to underpin its practicability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have stimulated intensive research for optical and electrochemical (bio)sensing because of their low-cost production, tunable surface and optical properties [1,2]. The fluorescence (FL) sensing mechanism based on CDs generally involves photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and/or an inner filter effect (IFE) [1,3]. The analyte and the functional group on the surface of CDs interact either covalently or non-covalently inducing a change of the FL emission of the CDs. The quenching (turn-off) or enhancement (turn-on) of the FL of nanoparticle is dependent on the concentration of the analyte and could, therefore, be used for sensing and analytical purposes. The interaction with the analyte and the FL response have been shown to be easily optimized (or tuned) by the proper engineering of the CDs surface is key for the interaction with the analyte and the FL response and can engineered by pre- or post-synthetic design [4,5].

Bottom-up synthetic approaches offer the possibility to produce nanoparticles with tailored functional groups or photophysical and electrochemical properties [6–11]. On the other hand, the surface can be further engineered via post-synthetic modifications by preparing customized hybrid materials that integrate diverse functionalities [10,12,13]. As-prepared CDs have shown sensitivity, selectivity for different metals [14–18]. In these cases, the choice of the precursors dictates the functional groups on the surface of CDs. For example, Qian and co-workers reported amino-rich CDs for the selective sensing of Ag+ and Fe3+ though a photoinduced electron transfer process. However, CDs usually present diverse functional groups on the surface that can establish multiple surface interactions making difficult either to predict or tune their sensing ability.

Post-synthetic routes offer a wider framework of strategies capable to improve the selectivity of the sensor [19–23]. For instance, Li and coworkers exploited the selective detection capability of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) by preparing a CD-based supramolecular assembly for the detection of Hg2+ ions [23]. Alternatively, the abundant surface functional groups of CDs can be used to prepare covalent hybrid materials with specific organic molecules capable of interactions with specific ions [19].

Cadmium is introduced as toxic metal and worldwide environmental pollutant with harmful effects on the human health and other living beings. Cadmium, a usual component found in the electronic devices, nickel–cadmium batteries and fossil fuel ignition, can be entered in the water sources by industrial waste and run-offs. Exposure to cadmium over tolerance limits for long time can result to serious health problems to human including renal dysfunction, osteoporosis and cancer. It can be transmitted to body using contaminated water, soil or foods that cause damages to the nervous, alimentary and hemopoietic systems [24–27].

Herein we report a CD-based sensor obtained by post-synthetic covalent functionalization of CDs with a selective crown ligand recognition unit for Cd2+ions. Cadmium ions were targeted for two main reasons: (i) cadmium is a health hazardous pollutant and its selective detection is important to prevent human exposure; (ii) optical sensing platforms based on nanomaterials for cadmium detection are advantageous over common techniques in terms of operational simplicity and cost [28,29]. Also, despite the various successful reports of CD-based probe for metal sensing,[18] the use of CDs for the selective detection of cadmium ions has been minimally investigated [30–32] This work details the selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ ions via the fluorescence quenching of CDs by taking advantage of the well-known coordination chemistry of Cd2+ ions with crown ligand in preparing a covalent hybrid material [28,33–36].

L-Arginine (Arg, >98%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5%), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), 2-Aminomethyl-18-crown-6 (Crown, 95%), Meldrum acid, 4-(dimethylamino)pyridine (DMAP), lead(II) nitrate, potassium nitrate, cadmium(II) nitrate, nickel(II) nitrate, cobalt(II) nitrate and chromium(II) chloride hydrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Dialysis membrane tubes (0.5–1 kDa) were bought from Spectrum Labs. Ultrapure fresh water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (>18 MΩ Milli-Q, Millipore) was used in all experiments. CDs were prepared according to our microwave assisted hydrothermal process using Arg and EDA as carbon and nitrogen sources [37].

The microwave synthesis was performed using a CEM Discover-SP microwave reactor. Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) was performed using a column packed with Sephadex LH-20 and methanol as eluent. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were acquired on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Perkin Elmer 2000 FT-IR spectrometer (KBr).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained with a Nanoscope IIIa, VEECO Instrument. As a general procedure to perform AFM analyses, tapping mode with a HQ:NSC19/ALBS probe (80 kHz; 0.6 N/m) (MikroMasch) from drop cast of samples in an aqueous solution (concentration of few mg/mL) on a mica substrate was performed. The obtained AFM-images were analyzed in using Gwyddion 2.35.

The Stern − Volmer plot was obtained according to the equation:[38]
(1)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
M
where I0 and I are referred to the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Crown in absence and presence of quencher respectively, KSV is the constant of Stern − Volmer and M indicates the molar concentration of the analyte (in this case Cd2+ ions).

The lower detection limit was calculated by the following equation:[38]
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
B
K
SV
where σB is the standard deviation of fluorescence emission intensity of the blank sensor sample. The standard deviation of the LOD was calculated according to the equation:[38]
(3)
σ
LOD
=
σ B
I 0
¯

CDs were prepared according to our previously published procedure using reported protocol from Arginine (Arg) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as carbon and nitrogen sources [39]. Briefly, Arg (87.0 mg, 0.5 mmol), EDA (33.0 μL, 0.5 mmol) and 100 μL of Milli-Q water heated at 240 °C and 26 bar for 180 s. The deep yellow solution was filtered via using a 0.1 μm microporous membrane and then, dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff 0.5–1 KDa) against water for 2 days. The solution was lyophilized giving a brownish solid (25.0 mg). The fluorescence quantum yield was obtained to be 26%.

The amino groups on the surface of CDs were modified in carboxylic acid by using Meldrum’s acid (2,2-dimethyl-1,3-dioxane-4,6-dione). CDs (25.0 mg, 1350 μmol g−1 of amino groups) were dissolved in 5.0 mL of anhydrous DMF, then Meldrum’s acid (23.9 mg, 0.17 mmol) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (2.5 mg, 292 mmol) were added. The mixture was stirred under argon at 80 °C for 48 h. The volatiles were removed under high vacuum, the residue was taken-up in methanol and purified through SEC. The blue luminescent fractions were collected, dried under vacuum and the obtained residue was lyophilized giving a brownish solid (CDs-COOH, ca. 17 mg).

The carboxylic functional groups on the surface of CDs-COOH were further reacted with the amino functionalities of Crown adapting our procedure for the carbodiimide condensation reaction[37]. To a solution of CDs-COOH (40.0 mg) in anhydrous DMF (5.0 mL) EDC (30.0 mg, 0.2 mmol) and NHS (18.0 mg, 0.2 mmol) were added under stirring at room temperature. After 1 h, Crown (13.0 μL, 0.1 mmol) was added and the solution was stirred in the same conditions overnight. The solvent was removed under reduced pressure and the residue was dissolved in methanol and purified trough SEC. The obtained fractions were collected, dried, and the product was finally taken-up in water and lyophilized giving a yellow solid (CDs-Crown, ca. 32 mg). The fluorescence quantum yield was 22%.

For Cd2+ ions sensing, a solution of CDs-Crown (1.0 mg) in Milli-Q water (3.0 mL, pH = 7) was used. The fluorescence emission spectra were collected by exciting at 300 nm. The same method was used for the other transition or heavy metal ions (K+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Co2+ and Ni2+) and when tap and river waters (previously filtered, 0.2 μm) were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the hybrid material
ce:section.ce:para: We settled on our synthetic protocol by using nitrogen rich precursors (arginine and ethylene diamine) to obtain blue emissive nitrogen-doped CDs in a microwave assisted hydrothermal process [37,39]. The hydrophilic nature of CDs makes them an ideal platform for environmental water analysis. Ethylene diamine contributes to the formation of a shell rich in amino groups,[37,39] which have been exploited for the formation of a covalent hybrid material with the crown ether (CDs-Crown).

To synthetize the CDs-Crown sensor, a two-step synthetic procedure was employed (see Experimental for details): (i) the amines on the surface of CDs first reacted with Meldrum acid forming a surface rich in carboxylic groups (CDs-COOH); (ii) the crown was covalently attached to the CDs-COOH via a carbodiimide condensation reaction forming the hybrid material CDs-Crown (Fig. 1 ).

The size and morphology of dots were analyzed by AFM (Fig. 3
a and 3b). By statistical analysis of the height of about three hundred nanoparticles, average sizes of CDs and CDs-Crown were determined about 2.5 and 5.5 nm, respectively. The TEM image in Fig. S4 confirms the size (5–6 nm) of CDs-Crown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the hybrid material for the detection of cadmium (II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence studies of the CDs-crown showed a significant decrease in the presence of cadmium ions. The sensing performance of CDs-Crown was firstly optimized by monitoring the emission intensity of the emission peak at 360 nm (at the optimal excitation wavelength of 300 nm) as a function of the pH and the reaction time (Fig. 4 ). The optimum condition including pH 6–8 and 10 min of reaction time led to the higher quenching of the fluorescence of the sensor, probably it is due to the cadmium ions are presumably in the more oxidized state and forming oxide or hydroxide species at higher values of pH [41]. We therefore used and a reaction time of 10 min and pH 7 as optimize conditions for the determination of Cd2+ ions. We hypothesize that the “turn-off” fluorescence of the hybrid in presence of the cadmium ions relies on a photoinduced electron transfer sensing mechanism.[42] In this process, the crown ether acts as recognition site and the binding with Cd2+ induces the PET process from the photoexcited CDs to the empty d orbits of Cd2+ that weaken the emission from the hybrid material.

The nanodots are rich in surface functionalities consisting in carboxylic groups and other oxygen- or nitrogen-based groups, which can coordinate with the metal ions.[43,44]. We therefore compared the sensing ability of the hybrid material to the one of the bare CDs-COOH at the same experimental conditions, i.e. upon the addition of 100 μM cadmium ion at pH 7. Fig. 5
a shows that the fluorescence of the hybrid material is quenched to a greater degree (a factor of almost two) than that of the nanoparticle alone. Also, the quenching of the fluorescence of the hybrid material upon the addition of Cd2+ ions showed a lower dependence on the pH of the solution and required a lower reaction time than that of the nanoparticle alone ( Fig. S5 ).

Fig. 5 b and S5 show the fluorescence responses of CDs-Crown and CDs-COOH by increasing the metal ion concentrations. At the optimized conditions of pH 7, 10 min of incubation and 300 nm as excitation wavelength, the FL intensity decreases, and the degree of quenching reaches a plateau at a concentration of 120 μM. When the concentration of Cd2+ ions was 100 μM, 9% of the FL intensity of CDs-COOH was quenched while>50% of the FL intensity of the hybrid material was quenched, thereby showing that the hybrid material has a higher sensitivity than the dots alone. The hybrid showed a good linear response of the relative fluorescence intensity with respect to the Cd2+ ions concentration in the range of 1.0–120 µM (R = 0.98) (Fig. 5 c) with a limit of detection of 0.05 μM (with a standard deviation of 3.6 %) that is in the range of other sensing platforms (either of a molecular or a nanoscale level) [45–53].

The selectivity of nanohybrid evaluated with other environmentally relevant ions using our optimized experimental conditions. Under our optimized experimental conditions, the hybrid material showed high selectivity for Cd2+ ions over other transition or heavy metal ions (K+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+, Fig. 6
and
Fig. S7 ). In comparison to Cd2+ ions, which showed a quenching of ca. 48%, the other tested ions showed a small decrease of the fluorescence intensity of the hybrid material (upon addition of 100 µM of analyte), reaching maximum a quenching of 4% in presence of Cr2+ ions, thereby indicating a good selectivity of the hybrid material.

This selectivity can be related to host–guest complexation which is created by electrostatic interaction between electron donors and acceptors and also, shape and size complementation between host (crown) and the gest (metal ions). Also, Fig. S8 shows no considerable changes in the fluorescence signal were detected by addition of different metal ions (K+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Zn2+ (100 µM)) compared with a signal of Cd2+ (100 µM).

Encouraged by these findings, we wanted to investigate more in deep the ability of the sensor CDs-Crown to discriminate Cd2+ ions in presence of other possible interfering or competitive analytes by evaluating its performance in the analysis of real samples (tap and river water). No Cd2+ ions were detected in in any of these samples and therefore the standard addition technique was used (Table 1 ).[54] Satisfactory recoveries of Cd2+ ions were obtained, namely 94–105% and 96–102% for river and tap water, respectively. These results suggest that our sensor can be potentially applied for the detection and quantification of Cd2+ ions in practical samples since it is selective even in presence of other possible interfering species.

In summary, we have reported a novel fluorescent sensor for Cd2+ ions by using a CDs-based hybrid material. We have synthesized and characterized a covalent hybrid material consisting of CDs and crown ether where the selective binding ability of the latter quench the fluorescence emission of the nanoparticle. Fluorescence studies in different metal ion environment or in real samples showed the feasibility of the CDs-crown material as a probe for Cd2+ ions detection. Overall, this work shows that the surface functionalization with selective organic ligand is a viable strategy for preparing hybrid material with improved sensing capability and can be extended to optically detect other metal ions by using CDs as probes. If compared to other reported Cd2+ sensors, our results indicate that CND-Crown could be reasonably considered a valid alternative sensor (Table 2 ).
Zeinab Saberi: writing, analysis, methodology, writing and validation. Behzad Rezaei: supervision, and editing draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The dots and materials were produced at the University of Trieste in the group of Prof. Maurizio Prato.The authors would like also to thank him and his group especially Francesca Arcudi and Michele Cacioppo. The IUT group greatly acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology of I.R. Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114809.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
